content.text: Modern programs often spend most of their time
— waiting for a file to load, a database to respond, or a web server to reply. Traditional Python runs these tasks one after another, meaning your program sits idle while it waits.
is Python's built-in way to make your program do something useful during that waiting time, without the complexity of threads.
are two keywords introduced in Python 3.5 that let you write
in a style that looks almost identical to regular synchronous code. Together, they form the foundation of Python's
— a special kind of function that can be paused and resumed.
is used inside a coroutine to pause execution at a specific point and hand control back to the event loop until a result is ready.
The underlying engine that makes all of this work is the
— a scheduler that decides which coroutine runs next.
. Consider fetching data from 100 URLs. Synchronous code fetches them one at a time:
, all 100 requests are sent out, and the program handles each response as it arrives:
All 100 URLs sent → responses arrive as ready → total: ~200–400ms
This matters enormously in web servers, APIs, data pipelines, bots, scrapers, and any program that talks to external services.
Thousands of coroutines can run concurrently on a single thread, each consuming far less memory than a thread.
No race conditions from shared memory.
Because only one coroutine runs at a time (cooperative multitasking), you avoid the classic threading bugs caused by simultaneous memory access.
Unlike callback-based async (seen in older JavaScript or Twisted),
lets you write top-to-bottom code that is easy to reason about.
You decide exactly where your function can pause, making the execution model explicit and predictable.
If your program is CPU-heavy (image processing, machine learning, number crunching),
in a function, that function must be
. This cascades upward through your call stack — you cannot
Not all libraries support async. Using a blocking library (like the standard
) inside a coroutine will block the entire event loop, defeating the purpose.
Stack traces in async code can be less obvious, and errors in unawaited coroutines can silently disappear.
The mental model of cooperative multitasking is new to most developers coming from synchronous Python.
The event loop is the heart of
. It is a continuous loop that:
Picks the next coroutine that is ready to run.
Runs it until it hits an
Suspends that coroutine and moves to another ready one.
Comes back when the awaited result is available.
points, unlike threads which can be interrupted at any moment.
A coroutine is what you get when you call an
function. Importantly, calling a coroutine function does

async def greet():
    print("Hello")

result = greet()   # Nothing prints yet — this is just a coroutine object
To actually run it, you must either
it (from inside another coroutine) or pass it to the event loop.
"pause here and wait for this result."
"I'm free — run something else while I wait."

import asyncio

async def fetch_data():
    print("Fetching...")
    await asyncio.sleep(2)   # Pause here; event loop runs other coroutines
    print("Done!")
    return {"data": 42}

async def main():
    result = await fetch_data()
    print(result)

asyncio.run(main())
, it does not block the event loop — it simply schedules a resume after 2 seconds.
, the event loop is free to run any other coroutine that is ready. This is what enables concurrency on a single thread.
Making HTTP requests to APIs or web pages (use
Building web servers or APIs (FastAPI, Starlette, aiohttp server)
Writing chat bots, WebSocket handlers, or real-time systems
Running many I/O tasks concurrently (fetching 100 URLs, querying 50 endpoints)
The libraries you need are synchronous only and you cannot switch
The program is simple and sequential with only one or two I/O calls — the overhead is not worth it
You need compatibility with Python 2 (not supported)
to call other coroutines or async operations
Step 3 — Run the event loop with
creates a new event loop, runs your top-level coroutine, then closes the loop. It is the standard entry point for any async program in Python 3.7+.
Step 4 — Run multiple coroutines concurrently with

import asyncio

async def task(name, delay):
    print(f"{name} started")
    await asyncio.sleep(delay)
    print(f"{name} done")

async def main():
    await asyncio.gather(
        task("A", 2),
        task("B", 1),
        task("C", 3),
    )

asyncio.run(main())

A started
B started
C started
B done      ← finishes first (1 second)
A done      ← finishes second (2 seconds)
C done      ← finishes last (3 seconds)
Total time: ~3 seconds, not 6
runs all coroutines concurrently and waits for all of them to finish. This is one of the most commonly used patterns.

import asyncio

async def fetch(url):
    print(f"Fetching {url}")
    await asyncio.sleep(1)   # Simulates network delay
    return f"Data from {url}"

async def main():
    r1 = await fetch("url1")
    r2 = await fetch("url2")
    r3 = await fetch("url3")
    print(r1, r2, r3)
    # Total: ~3 seconds

asyncio.run(main())

async def main():
    results = await asyncio.gather(
        fetch("url1"),
        fetch("url2"),
        fetch("url3"),
    )
    print(results)
    # Total: ~1 second

asyncio.run(main())
schedules a coroutine to run and returns a

import asyncio

async def background_job():
    await asyncio.sleep(1)
    print("Background job done")

async def main():
    task = asyncio.create_task(background_job())   # Starts immediately in background
    print("Doing other work...")
    await asyncio.sleep(0.5)
    print("Still doing other work...")
    await task   # Wait for background job to finish
    print("All done")

asyncio.run(main())

Doing other work...
Still doing other work...
Background job done
All done

import asyncio

async def slow_operation():
    await asyncio.sleep(10)
    return "result"

async def main():
    try:
        result = await asyncio.wait_for(slow_operation(), timeout=3.0)
    except asyncio.TimeoutError:
        print("Operation timed out!")

asyncio.run(main())
Real-World Example: Fetching Multiple APIs Concurrently
A realistic scenario: a dashboard backend that must fetch user data, order history, and inventory status from three separate microservices before rendering a response.

import asyncio
import aiohttp

async def fetch_json(session, url):
    async with session.get(url) as response:
        return await response.json()

async def get_dashboard_data(user_id):
    urls = {
        "user":      f"https://api.example.com/users/{user_id}",
        "orders":    f"https://api.example.com/orders?user={user_id}",
        "inventory": f"https://api.example.com/inventory/status",
    }

    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        user, orders, inventory = await asyncio.gather(
            fetch_json(session, urls["user"]),
            fetch_json(session, urls["orders"]),
            fetch_json(session, urls["inventory"]),
        )

    return {"user": user, "orders": orders, "inventory": inventory}

async def main():
    data = await get_dashboard_data(user_id=42)
    print(data)

asyncio.run(main())
Without async, three sequential API calls at 300ms each would take ~900ms. With
, all three fire simultaneously and the total time is ~300ms — the time of the slowest single call.
At production scale, a FastAPI server built on this pattern can handle thousands of such concurrent requests on a single worker process, each coroutine independently managing its own I/O waits.

# WRONG — blocks the entire event loop
async def bad():
    time.sleep(2)

# CORRECT
async def good():
    await asyncio.sleep(2)
is a blocking call. Inside a coroutine, it freezes the whole event loop for 2 seconds, preventing every other coroutine from running.

async def main():
    fetch_data()   # No await — this creates a coroutine object but never runs it
. The function body simply never executes.

# WRONG — inside a Jupyter notebook or an existing async context
asyncio.run(main())   # Raises RuntimeError
In environments with a running loop (like Jupyter), use

import requests   # Synchronous — will block the event loop

async def bad_fetch(url):
    return requests.get(url)   # Blocks while waiting for HTTP response
5. Running CPU-heavy code inside a coroutine

async def process_image(data):
    result = heavy_computation(data)   # Blocks for 3 seconds — freezes event loop
    return result
For CPU-bound work, offload to a thread or process pool:

import asyncio

async def process_image(data):
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    result = await loop.run_in_executor(None, heavy_computation, data)
    return result
as the single entry point. Never scatter
When you have multiple independent I/O tasks, gather them rather than awaiting them sequentially.
When a background task should start immediately but you don't need to wait for it right away, create a task and await it later.
Always await tasks before your program exits.
Unawaited tasks that are garbage-collected will emit warnings. Keep references to tasks and await or cancel them cleanly.
for async context managers and iterators.
to properly open and close connections.

async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
    ...   # Session is properly closed even if an error occurs
Add timeouts to every external call.
Network calls can hang indefinitely. Wrap them with
to enforce a maximum wait time.
Do not mix sync and async code carelessly.
If you must call a blocking function, always use
to run it in a thread pool so the event loop stays free.

A single async Python process can handle tens of thousands of concurrent connections, something that would require thousands of threads (and gigabytes of RAM) in a traditional model. Each coroutine stack is far lighter than a thread — typically a few kilobytes versus megabytes per thread.
However, performance depends on the event loop not being blocked. A single blocking call (a
, a CPU-heavy loop, a synchronous DB query) can freeze all concurrent coroutines. In high-throughput systems:
as a drop-in replacement for the default event loop. It is a Cython-based implementation built on
(the same engine as Node.js) and can be 2–4× faster for I/O-intensive workloads.
) to scale across CPU cores. Each worker runs its own event loop, so CPU cores are fully utilized.
) to detect slow coroutines and blocked callbacks.

# Enabling debug mode programmatically
asyncio.run(main(), debug=True)
is essential for testing async code — it allows
test functions and manages the event loop lifecycle during tests.
is the modern standard for I/O-bound Python code. It beats threading for raw concurrency numbers and code clarity, but yields to
raises an exception and you never
it, the exception is silently discarded (with a warning in Python 3.9+). Always attach error handling or await all tasks.

async def risky():
    raise ValueError("oops")

async def main():
    task = asyncio.create_task(risky())
    # If we never await task, the ValueError disappears silently
    await task   # This will properly raise the exception
point. Always handle this in long-running coroutines using

async def long_task():
    try:
        await asyncio.sleep(100)
    except asyncio.CancelledError:
        print("Cleaning up...")
        raise   # Always re-raise CancelledError
    finally:
        release_resources()
blocks ensure that connections, file handles, and locks are always released even when exceptions occur. Prefer
over manually opening and closing async resources.
are the async equivalents of threading primitives. Using a regular
inside a coroutine will deadlock the event loop. Always use
Think of the event loop as a

managing multiple orders at once. The chef starts cooking Order A, puts it in the oven (an I/O wait), then immediately starts chopping vegetables for Order B. When the oven timer goes off, the chef goes back to Order A. No time is wasted standing in front of the oven.
— the chef pauses that task and does something else.
is the event loop — only one thing is actively happening at any moment, but many things are in progress simultaneously.
"stop this coroutine and let others run."
defines a coroutine — a function that can be paused and resumed at
suspends the current coroutine and yields control to the event loop, which runs other ready coroutines.
The event loop is a single-threaded scheduler — only one coroutine runs at a time, but many are in progress concurrently.
is the primary tool for running multiple coroutines in parallel.
only helps with I/O-bound work — for CPU-bound tasks, use
